food_subgroup: Noodle dishes with mixed seafood and protein toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai combo vermicelli with mixed seafood. FOODON_00002187 (noodle dish) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC (noodle-based composite dishes) is the closest defensible parent. No specific Thai seafood vermicelli combo code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Thai combo vermicelli dish. SNOMED CT does not enumerate individual composite noodle dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, representing a full bowl of Super Combo Vermicelli as typically served at a Thai seafood restaurant in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Mee Hoon (Rice Vermicelli) with Mixed Seafood — composite estimate derived from USDA FoodData Central (Rice noodles, cooked, FDC ID 168930) and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for Southeast Asia (seafood components, prawn, fish cake)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / FAO INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Super Combo Vermicelli exists in any approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of: cooked rice vermicelli (~55% by weight, USDA FDC 168930), mixed prawns (~20%, FAO/INFOODS SEA), fish cake (~10%, FAO/INFOODS SEA), vegetables and sauce (~15%, USDA FDC generic). Values represent a plausible per-100g profile for a mixed Thai seafood vermicelli dish. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles (cooked) — GI value from University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to Super Combo Vermicelli
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Super Combo Vermicelli. GI of 53 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI ~53, low category), which is the primary carbohydrate vehicle in this dish. The mixed seafood and vegetable components do not materially raise the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (53 × 71.2 g available carbohydrate per 400 g serving) / 100 = 37.7, rounded to 37.9. This is a high GL per serving due to the large portion size, consistent with a full noodle meal.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, oyster sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (1,680 mg per full serving), which represents a substantial proportion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg; requesting reduced sauce or sharing a portion is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index (53) but high glycemic load per full serving due to large portion of rice vermicelli
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that while the GI of rice vermicelli is moderate-low (53), a full 400 g serving delivers a high glycemic load (~38 GL units); portion control to half a serving and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and protein can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from mixed seafood, partially offset by low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the high sodium content (approximately 1,680 mg per serving) is the primary concern; the saturated fat content is relatively low at 0.5 g per 100 g, and the inclusion of prawns and seafood provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids, but sodium reduction through sauce modification is strongly recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Super Combo Vermicelli is a Thai-style rice vermicelli noodle dish featuring a generous combination of mixed seafood toppings, typically including whole prawns, fish cake slices, and other shellfish or seafood, served in or alongside a flavourful broth or stir-fried sauce made with fish sauce, oyster sauce, garlic, and chilli. The dish is assembled from boiled or stir-fried rice vermicelli (mee hoon) as the carbohydrate base, topped with the seafood combo and garnished with bean sprouts, spring onion, and fresh chilli. The texture contrasts soft, silky noodles with the firm bite of prawns and the springy chew of fish cake. The flavour profile is savoury, umami-rich, mildly spicy, and aromatic. In Singapore, this dish is served at Thai seafood restaurants and hawker-adjacent establishments, often as a premium sharing or individual meal. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~7 g per 100 g) from the seafood components, a moderate carbohydrate load from the rice vermicelli, and is relatively low in saturated fat. However, the sodium content is high due to the sauce base, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index of the rice vermicelli base is approximately 53 (low-medium), but the glycemic load per full serving is high due to portion size, which is relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with crustacean or fish allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Super Combo Vermicelli?
Is Super Combo Vermicelli suitable for diabetics?
What seafood is in Super Combo Vermicelli?
Is Super Combo Vermicelli high in sodium?
What is the GI of vermicelli noodles?
Can I eat Super Combo Vermicelli if I have high blood pressure?

fish cake: ลูกชิ้นปลา

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Super Combo Vermicelli exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are estimated by weighted composite of approved-source components (cooked rice vermicelli from USDA FDC, seafood components from FAO/INFOODS SEA). GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates; no single approved-source entry covers this exact dish. Values should be treated as indicative and updated if an exact match is identified in HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA in future. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite estimate. 3. vitamin_e_mg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite estimate. 4. selenium_mcg is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this composite estimate. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed zero as rice vermicelli and seafood components contain negligible industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FDC source data. 6. The price of SGD 50.80 from the input record is noted; this is unusually high for a single vermicelli dish and may reflect a large sharing platter or premium seafood combo at Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay; this does not affect nutrient estimation. 7. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Shrimp Prawn Seafood - Boat Quay) is not confirmed halal-certified; this should be verified. 8. GI set to 53 based on analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (cooked rice noodles); glycemic load per serving calculated for a 400 g serving: (53 × 71.2 g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 37.9 GL units.
